name: Mock Test - 08
instruction: This question paper contains three sections – A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. 3 . Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: Let f(x) = x 2 then, dom (f) and range (f) are respectively
questions.question_html: Let f(x) = x2 then, dom (f) and range (f) are respectively
questions.explanation: Domain of f = R and Range of f = R + U {0}

questions.question_text: In linear programming, optimal solution
questions.question_html: In linear programming, optimal solution

options.text: satisfies all the constraints as well as the objective function
options.html: satisfies all the constraints as well as the objective function

questions.correct_option_text: satisfies all the constraints as well as the objective function
questions.explanation: In linear programming, any point in the feasible region which gives that gives the optimal value (maximum or minimum) of the objective function is called optimal solution. In other words, it satisfies all the constraints as well as the objective function.

questions.question_text: If f (x) = 2x and {tex}g(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+1{/tex} , then which of the following can be a discontinuous function
questions.question_html: If f (x) = 2x and {tex}g(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+1{/tex}, then which of the following can be a discontinuous function
questions.explanation: We know that, if f and g be continuous functions, then g is continuous at these points, where {tex}g(x) \ne 0{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac{{g(x)}}{{f(x)}} = \frac{{\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + 1}}{{2x}} = \frac{{{x^2} + 2}}{{4x}}{/tex} Which is discontinuous at x = 0.

questions.question_text: Any tangent to the curve y = 2x 7 + 3x + 5
questions.question_html: Any tangent to the curve y = 2x7 + 3x + 5

options.text: makes an acute angle with x-axis
options.html: makes an acute angle with x-axis

options.text: makes an obtuse angle with x-axis
options.html: makes an obtuse angle with x-axis

questions.correct_option_text: makes an acute angle with x-axis
questions.explanation: y = 2x 7 + 3x + 5 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 14x 6 + 3 Even power is always positive. Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}>0{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta>0{/tex} Hence, tangent makes an acute angle with x-axis to the curve.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: The set of all feasible solutions of a LPP is not a converse set
options.html: The set of all feasible solutions of a LPP is not a converse set

options.text: Every LPP admits an optimal solution
options.html: Every LPP admits an optimal solution

options.text: If an LPP admits two optimal solutions it has an infinite number of optimal solutions
options.html: If an LPP admits two optimal solutions it has an infinite number of optimal solutions

options.text: A LPP admits unique optimal solution
options.html: A LPP admits unique optimal solution

questions.correct_option_text: If an LPP admits two optimal solutions it has an infinite number of optimal solutions
questions.explanation: It is known that the optimal solution of an LPP either exists uniquely, does not exist or exists infinitely. So, If a LPP admits two optimal solution it has an infinite number of optimal solutions

questions.question_text: A square matrix A = [a ij ] nxn is called a diagonal matrix if a ij = 0 for
questions.question_html: A square matrix A = [aij]nxn is called a diagonal matrix if aij = 0 for
questions.explanation: In a diagonal matrix all elements except diagonal elements are zero.i.e. a ij = 0 , if i {tex}\ne{/tex} j.

questions.question_text: If y = sec -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = sec-1 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sec ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec y=\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos y = 2x 2 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos -1 (2x 2 - 1) Put x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ,we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y =cos -1 (2cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y =cos -1 (cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} But {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos -1 x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=2 \cdot \frac{d\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=2 \cdot\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The system of equations, x + y = 2 and 2x + 2y = 3 has
questions.question_html: The system of equations, x + y = 2 and 2x + 2y = 3 has
questions.explanation: For No solution, {tex}\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{{{b_1}}}{{{b_2}}} \ne \frac{{{c_1}}}{{{c_2}}}{/tex} , for given system of equations we have: {tex}\frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \ne \frac{2}{3}{/tex} .

questions.question_text: The feasible solution for a LPP is shown in Figure. Let Z = 3x – 4y be the objective function. (Maximum value of Z + Minimum value of Z) is equal to
questions.question_html: The feasible solution for a LPP is shown in Figure. Let Z = 3x – 4y be the objective function. (Maximum value of Z + Minimum value of Z) is equal to
questions.explanation: Corner points Z = 3x - 4y (0, 0 ) 0 (5,0) 15(Max.) (6,8 ) -14 (6 , 5 ) -2 (4,10) -28 (0,8) -32……………..(Min.) Maximum value of Z + Minimum value of Z = 15 + ( -32) = - 17 .

questions.question_text: A cylindrical vessel of radius 0.5 m is filled with oil at the rate of 0.25 {tex}\pi{/tex} m 3 /minute. The rate at which the surface of the oil is rising, is
questions.question_html: A cylindrical vessel of radius 0.5 m is filled with oil at the rate of 0.25{tex}\pi{/tex}m3/minute. The rate at which the surface of the oil is rising, is
questions.explanation: {tex}V=\pi r^{2} h{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=\pi r^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} h}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{\pi r^{2}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{1}{\pi(0.5)^{2}} \times(0.25) \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{min}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: The number of points at which the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{x-[x]}{/tex} is not continuous is
questions.question_html: The number of points at which the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{x-[x]}{/tex} is not continuous is
questions.explanation: Given, f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x-[x]}{/tex} When x is an integer, x - [x] = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} For all x {tex}\in I{/tex} , function is not defined. Hence, all x {tex}\in I{/tex} , the set of points where the function is not continuous.

questions.question_text: Maximise Z = 3x + 4y subject to the constraints: x + y ≤ 4, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Maximise Z = 3x + 4y subject to the constraints: x + y ≤ 4, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = 3x + 4 y ……(1). The given constraints are : x + y ≤ 4, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. The corner points obtained by constructing the line x+ y= 4, are (0,0),(0,4) and (4,0). Corner points Z = 3x +4y O ( 0 ,0 ) Z = 3(0)+4(0) = 0 A ( 4 , 0 ) Z = 3(4) + 4 (0) = 12 B ( 0 , 4 ) Z = 3(0) + 4 ( 4) = 16 …( Max. ) therefore Z = 16 is maximum at ( 0 , 4 ) .

questions.question_text: Minimum value of the function f(x) = x 2 + x + 1 is
questions.question_html: Minimum value of the function f(x) = x2 + x + 1 is
questions.explanation: Given, f(x) = x 2 + x +1 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f'(x) = 2x + 1 For minimum value of f(x) we have f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} Now, f''(x) = 2 >0, hence the minimum of f(x) exist at x = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} and minimum value = f( {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right)=\log a{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right)=\log a{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: We have, {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right)=\log a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\sin \log a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(2 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)-\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 x^{3}-2 x^{2} y \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y^{2}-2 y^{3} \frac{d y}{d x}-2 x^{3}-2 x^{2} y \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y^{2}+2 y^{3} \frac{d y}{d x}}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-4 x^{2} y \frac{d y}{d x}+4 x y^{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-4 x^{2} y \frac{d y}{d x}=-4 x y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{4 x y^{2}}{4 x^{2} y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: If {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\log _{e}\left(e / x^{2}\right)}{\log _{e}\left(e x^{2}\right)}\right\}+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+2 \log _{e} x}{1-6 \log _{e} x}\right){/tex} , then {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} =
questions.question_html: If {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\log _{e}\left(e / x^{2}\right)}{\log _{e}\left(e x^{2}\right)}\right\}+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+2 \log _{e} x}{1-6 \log _{e} x}\right){/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} =
questions.explanation: {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\log _{e}\left(e / x^{2}\right)}{\log _{e}\left(e x^{2}\right)}\right\}+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+2 \log _{e} x}{1-6 \log _{e} x}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{1-2 \log _{e} \times}{1+2 \log _{e} \times}\right\}+\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{3+2 \log _{e} \times}{1-6 \log _{e} \times}\right\}{/tex} {tex}={/tex} tan -1 - tan -1 (2 log e x) + tan -1 (3) + tan -1 (2 log e x) {tex}={/tex} tan -1 1 + tan -1 (3) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}

questions.question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cr} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then A T + A = I 2 , if
questions.question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cr} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then AT + A = I2, if
questions.explanation: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} A + A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 2Cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 Cos {tex}\theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2n {tex}\pi+\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {n {tex}\in{/tex} Z}

questions.question_text: Find the approximate change in the volume V of a cube of side x metres caused by increasing the side by 1%
questions.question_html: Find the approximate change in the volume V of a cube of side x metres caused by increasing the side by 1%
questions.explanation: Given, the side of the cube is x We have volume of a cube = V = x 3 Now, {tex} dV=\left( \frac { dV }{ dx } \right) \triangle x=3{ x }^{ 2 }\triangle x{/tex} Change in th side = {tex}\Delta x{/tex} = 0.01x Thus, the approximate change in volume = dV = 3x 2 ( 0.01 x) = 0.03 x 3 m 3 therefore, volume is increased approximately by 3 percentage.

questions.question_text: If {tex}\;x + y = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} then (1 + tanx)(1 + tany) is equal to
questions.question_html: If{tex}\;x + y = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} then (1 + tanx)(1 + tany) is equal to
questions.explanation: If {tex}\;x + y = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} then (1 + tanx)(1 + tany) {tex} \Rightarrow (1 + \tan x)(1 + \tan \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} - x} \right)){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow (1 + \tan x)\left( {1 + \frac{{1 - \tan x}}{{1 + tanx}}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow (1 + \tan x)\left( {\frac{2}{{1 + \tan x}}} \right) = 2{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\sin x}}{{2x}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} (Using L’hospital Rule ).

questions.question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then A n (where n {tex}\in{/tex} N) equals
questions.question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then An (where n {tex}\in{/tex} N) equals
questions.explanation: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A n = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \times{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & a \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {n times, (where n {tex}\in{/tex} N)} A n = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & \text { na } \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let T be the set of all triangles in the Euclidean plane, and let a relation R on T be defined as aRb if a is congruent to b {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} T. Then R is
questions.question_html: Let T be the set of all triangles in the Euclidean plane, and let a relation R on T be defined as aRb if a is congruent to b {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} T. Then R is
questions.explanation: Given that, R be a relation on T defined as aRb if a is congruent to b {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} T Now, aRa {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a is congruent to a, which is true since every triangle is congruent to itself. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a {tex}\in{/tex} T {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive. Let aRb {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a is congruent to b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b is congruent to a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} bRa {tex}\therefore{/tex} (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} T {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is symmetric. Let aRb {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a is congruent to b and bRc {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b is congruent to c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a is congruent to c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} aRc {tex}\therefore{/tex} (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b,c {tex}\in{/tex} T {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive. Hence, R is an equivalence relation.

questions.question_text: The normal at the point (1,1) on the curve {tex}2y + x^2 = 3{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The normal at the point (1,1) on the curve {tex}2y + x^2 = 3{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Equation of curve is {tex}2y + x^2 = 3{/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-x{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{(1,1)}=-1{/tex} i.e, slope of the tangent at (1, 1) is (-1) Therefore, the slope of the normal to the given curve at point (1,1) is, {tex}\frac{{ - 1}}{{{{\left. {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]}_{(1,1)}}}} = 1{/tex} Thus, the equation of the normal to the curve at (1,1) is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y – 1 =1(x - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 1 = x – 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x – y = 0. Which is the required solution

questions.question_text: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem, and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then
questions.question_html: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem, and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then

options.text: the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R
options.html: the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R

options.text: the objective function Z has only a minimum value on R
options.html: the objective function Z has only a minimum value on R

options.text: the objective function Z has only a maximum value on R
options.html: the objective function Z has only a maximum value on R

options.text: the objective function Z has no minimum value on R
options.html: the objective function Z has no minimum value on R

questions.correct_option_text: the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R
questions.explanation: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem, and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R and each of these occur at the corner point (vertex) of R.

questions.question_text: The value of k for which f(x) {tex}=\left\{\begin{array}{r} \frac{\sin 5 x}{3 x}, \text { if } x \neq 0 \\k, \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 is
questions.question_html: The value of k for which f(x) {tex}=\left\{\begin{array}{r} \frac{\sin 5 x}{3 x}, \text { if } x \neq 0 \\k, \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 is
questions.explanation: Since f(x) is continuous on 0,then we {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 5 x}{3 x}=f(0){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 5 x}{3 x} \times \frac{5 x}{5 x}=f(0){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 5 x}{5 x} \times \frac{5 x}{3 x}=f(0){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)=\frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x - \sin 2x}}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x - \sin 2x}}{{{x^3}}}{/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x - \sin 2x}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x(1 - \cos x)}}{{{x^3}}} = 2\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\sin x}}{x}.\frac{{1 - \operatorname{cosx} }}{{{x^2}}}= 2\times1\times\frac{1}{2} = 1{/tex}

questions.question_text: sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{4}{5}\right){/tex} = ?
questions.question_html: sin{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{4}{5}\right){/tex} = ?
questions.explanation: The given equation is sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{4}{5}\right){/tex} Let x = cos -1 {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} cos x = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} Therefore sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{4}{5}\right){/tex} becomes sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} x\right){/tex} , i.e sin( {tex}\frac x 2{/tex} ) We know that sin( {tex}\frac x 2{/tex} ) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1-\cos x}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{1-\frac{4}{5}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{\frac{1}{5}}{2}}{/tex} sin {tex}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If A = { 1, 2, 3}, then the relation R = {(1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)} in A is _
questions.question_html: If A = { 1, 2, 3}, then the relation R = {(1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)} in A is _
questions.explanation: A relation R on a non-empty set A is said to be transitive if xRy and y Rz {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} xRz, for all x {tex} \in {/tex} R. Here, (1, 2) and (2, 3) belongs to R implies that (1, 3) belongs to R.

questions.question_text: If xy + yz +zx = 1, then value of tan -1 x + tan -1 y + tan -1 z is
questions.question_html: If xy + yz +zx = 1, then value of tan-1x + tan-1y + tan-1z is
questions.explanation: {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x\, + \,\,{\tan ^{ - 1}}y\,\, + \,\,{\tan ^{ - 1}}z\,\\\,\, {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \,{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{x + y}}{{1 - xy}}} \right]\,\, + {\tan ^{ - 1}}z{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \,{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{\frac{{x + y}}{{1 - xy}} + z}}{{1 - \left( {\frac{{x + y}}{{1 - xy}}} \right)z}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \,\,{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{\frac{{x + y + z - xyz}}{{1 - xy}}}}{{\frac{{1 - xy - xz - yz}}{{1 - xy}}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \,\,{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{x + y + z - xyz}}{{1 - xy - xz - yz}}} \right]\,{/tex} {tex} = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{x + y + z - xyz}}{{1 - 1}}} \right]\,\,\, = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}[as \; xz\, + yz\, + xy\, = 1]{/tex}

questions.question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and kA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 3 a \\ 2 b & 24 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then the values of k, a, b, are respectively
questions.question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and kA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 3 a \\ 2 b & 24 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then the values of k, a, b, are respectively
questions.explanation: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and kA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 3 a \\ 2 b & 24 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 2 k \\ 3 k & -4 k \end{array}\right]{/tex} Comparing the equations, -4k = 24 k = -6 3k = 2b 3(-6) = 2b 2b = -18 b = -9 3a = 2k 3a = 2(-6) 3a = -12 a = -4 Values are k = -6, a = -4 & b = -9. Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: The point on the curve y = 12x - x 2 where the slope of the tangent is zero will be
questions.question_html: The point on the curve y = 12x - x2 where the slope of the tangent is zero will be
questions.explanation: y = 12x - x 2 Slope of the tangent = 0 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} 12 - 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 y = 12x - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 36 Point on curve is (6, 36)

questions.question_text: If {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} , then {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: If  {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}{/tex} put {tex}{x}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \mathbf{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {u}=\tan ^{-1}(\tan 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {u}=2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = 2 tan -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Let v {tex}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} Put x {tex}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=2 \tan ^{-1} \mathbf{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u}{d x} \times \frac{d x}{d v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}=\frac{2}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)} \times \frac{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{2}=1{/tex}

questions.question_text: If [x] stands for the integral part of x, then
questions.question_html: If [x] stands for the integral part of x, then
questions.explanation: The [x] is defined as the value which takes the nearest small integer in every integer interval. For example in the interval {tex}0≤x<1{/tex} the value of the function is 0 since it is the lowest integer in that interval . Hence {tex}\lim_{x\rightarrow1^+}{/tex} [x] [ since {tex}1\leq x<2{/tex} ] which gives the value as 1, which is the lowest integer of that interval.

questions.question_text: If V = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} , at what rate in cubic units is V increasing when r = 10 and {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=0.01 ?{/tex}
questions.question_html: If V = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex}, at what rate in cubic units is V increasing when r = 10 and {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}=0.01 ?{/tex}
questions.explanation: {tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to t, {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=\frac{4}{3} \times 3 \pi r^{2} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi(10)^{2} \times 0.01{/tex} {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \pi{/tex}

questions.question_text: tanx is periodic with period
questions.question_html: tanx is periodic with period
questions.explanation: The values of tanx repeats after an interval of {tex}\pi {/tex} .

questions.question_text: If the function f(x) {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{1-\cos 4 x}{8 x^{2}}, & x \neq 0 \\ k, x &=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is continuous x = 0 then k = ?
questions.question_html: If the function f(x) {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{1-\cos 4 x}{8 x^{2}}, & x \neq 0 \\ k, x &=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is continuous x = 0 then k = ?
questions.explanation: Here, given {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{1-\cos 4 x}{8 x^{2}}{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^{2} 2 x}{8 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^{2} 2 x}{2 \times 4 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin 2 x}{2 x}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 1

questions.question_text: In an LPP if the objective function Z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then every point on the line segment joining these two points give the same ________ value.
questions.question_html: In an LPP if the objective function Z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then every point on the line segment joining these two points give the same ________ value.
questions.explanation: In an LPP if the objective function Z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then every point on the line segment joining these two points give the same maximum value . If the problem has multiple optimal solutions at the corner points, then both the points will have the same (maximum or minimum)value.

questions.question_text: If {tex}f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {x-a} & {x-b} \\ {x+a} & {0} & {x-c} \\ {x+b} & {x+c} & {0} \end{array}\right|{/tex} , then
questions.question_html: If {tex}f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {x-a} & {x-b} \\ {x+a} & {0} & {x-c} \\ {x+b} & {x+c} & {0} \end{array}\right|{/tex}, then
questions.explanation: {tex}f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {x-a} & {x-b} \\ {x+a} & {0} & {x-c} \\ {x+b} & {x+c} & {0} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(0)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-a} & {-b} \\ {a} & {0} & {-c} \\ {b} & {c} & {0} \end{array}\right|{/tex} , which is skew - symmetric determinant of order 3 Hence f(0) = 0

questions.question_text: The set of points where the function f given by f (x) = {tex}|2 x-1|{/tex} sin x is differentiable is
questions.question_html: The set of points where the function f given by f (x) = {tex}|2 x-1|{/tex} sin x is differentiable is
questions.explanation: We have, {tex}f(x)=|2 x-1| \sin x{/tex} At {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , f(x) is not differentiable Hence, f(x), is differentiable in {tex}\mathbf{R}-\left\{\frac{1}{2}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\because R f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\underset{h\rightarrow0}{lim}~~ \frac{f\left(\frac{1}{2}+h\right)-f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left|2\left(\frac{1}{2}+h\right)-1\right| \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}+h\right)-0}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|2 h| \cdot \sin \left(\frac{1+2 h}{2}\right)}{h}=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}L f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\underset{h\rightarrow0}{lim} \frac{f\left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right)-f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left|2\left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right){-1}\right| \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right)-0}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|0-2 h|.\sin \left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right)}{-h}=-2 \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} Clearly, {tex} R f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \neq L f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} So, f(x) is not differentiable at {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let Z be the set of all integers and let R be a relation on Z defined by a Rb {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a {tex}\ge{/tex} b. Then, R is
questions.question_html: Let Z be the set of all integers and let R be a relation on Z defined by a Rb {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a {tex}\ge{/tex} b. Then, R is

options.text: symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
options.html: symmetric and transitive but not reflexive

options.text: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
options.html: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive

options.text: reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
options.html: reflexive and transitive but not symmetric

questions.correct_option_text: reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
questions.explanation: Here {tex}R=\{(a, b) | a \geq b, a, b \in Z\}{/tex} Reflexive: Clearly, a {tex}a \geq a \ \forall \ a \in Z{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is Reflexive Symmetric: {tex}(a, b) \in R \Rightarrow a \geq b{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad b \geq a{/tex} Hence R is not Symmetric Transitive: (a, ), (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow a \geq b{/tex} & {tex}b \geq c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a \geqslant c \Rightarrow\left(a_{0} c\right) \in R{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is Transitive. Hence R is Reflexive and Transitive but not symmetric.

questions.question_text: By graphical method, the solution of linear programming problem Maximize Z = 3x 1 + 5x 2 Subject to 3x 1 + 2x 2 {tex}\le{/tex} 1 8 x 1 {tex}\le{/tex} 4 x 2 {tex}\le{/tex} 6 x 1 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, x 2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, is
questions.question_html:
By graphical method, the solution of linear programming problem
Maximize Z = 3x1 + 5x2
Subject to 3x1 + 2x2 {tex}\le{/tex} 1 8
x1 {tex}\le{/tex} 4
x2 {tex}\le{/tex} 6
x1 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, x2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, is
questions.explanation: We need to maximize the function z = 3x 1 + 5 x 2 First, we will convert the given inequations into equations, we obtain the following equations: 3x 1 + 2x 2 = 18, x 1 = 4, x 2 = 6, x 1 = 0 and x 2 = 0 Region represented by 3x 1 + 2x 2 {tex}\le{/tex} 18 The line 3x 1 + 2x 2 = 18 meets the coordinate axes at A(6, 0) and B(0, 9) respectively. By joining these points we obtain the line 3x 1 + 2x 2 = 18 Clearly (0, 0) satisfies the inequation 3x 1 + 2x 2 = 18 . So, the region in the plane which contain the origin represents the solution set of the inequation 3x 1 + 2x 2 = 18 Region represented by x 1 {tex}\leq{/tex} 4 : The line x 1 = 4 is the line that passes through C(4, 0) and is parallel to the Y axis. The region to the left of the line x 1 = 4 will satisfy the inequation x 1 {tex}\leq{/tex} 4 Region represented by x 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 6 The line x 2 = 6 is the line that passes through D(0, 6) and is parallel to the x axis. The region below the line x 2 = 6 will satisfy the inequation x 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 6. Region represented by x 1 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 and x 2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 since, every point in the first quadrant satisfies these inequations. So, the first quadrant is the region represented by the inequations x 1 {tex}\geq{/tex} oand x 2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 The feasible region determined by the system of constraints, 3x 1 + 2x 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 18, x 1 {tex}\leq{/tex} 4, x 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 6, x 1 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, and x 2 {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, are as follows : Corner points are O(0, 0), D(0, 6), F(2, 6), E(4, 3) and C(4, 0). The values of the objective function at these points are given in the following table Points : Value of Z O(0, 0) : 3(0) + 5(0) = 0 D(0, 6) : 3(0) + 5(6) = 30 F(2, 6) : 3(2) + 5(6) = 36 E(4, 3) : 3(4) + 5(3) = 27 C(4, 0) : 3(4) + 5(0) = 12 We see that the maximum value of the objective function Z is 36 which is at F(2, 6)

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\frac{{x({e^{\sin }}^x - 1)}}{{1 - \cos x}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\frac{{x({e^{\sin }}^x - 1)}}{{1 - \cos x}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{x({e^{\sin x}} - 1)}}{{1 - \cos x}}\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\frac{{({e^{\sin x}} - 1)}}{x}}}{{\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{({e^{\sin x}} - 1)}}{{\sin x}}.\frac{{\sin x}}{x}.2 = 2 \left( {\because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}} \right) {/tex}

questions.question_text: If the graph of a differentiable function y = f (x) meets the lines y = – 1 and y = 1, then the graph

questions.correct_option_text: meets the line y = 0 at least once
questions.explanation: Since the graph cuts the lines y = -1 and y = 1, therefore, it must cut the line y = 0 at least once as the graph is a continuous curve in this case.

questions.question_text: Let T be the set of all triangles in a plane with R a relation on T given by R = {(T 1 ,T 2 ): T 1 is congruent to T 2 }. Then R is
questions.question_html: Let T be the set of all triangles in a plane with R a relation on T given by R = {(T1,T2): T1 is congruent to T2 }. Then R is
questions.explanation: Let T be the set of all triangles in a plane with R a relation on T given by R = {(T 1 , T 2 ): T 1 is congruent to T 2 }. (T 1 , T 2 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R if is T 1 congruent to T 2 . Reflexivity:T 1 {tex} \cong {/tex} T 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (T 1 , T 1 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R . Symmetry :(T 1 , T 2 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} T 1 {tex} \cong {/tex} T 2 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} T 2 {tex} \cong {/tex} T 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (T 2 , T 1 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R Transitivity :(T 1 , T 2 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R and (T 2 ,T 3 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} T 1 {tex} \cong {/tex} T 2 andT 2 {tex} \cong {/tex} {tex}{T_3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} T 1 {tex} \cong {/tex} T 2 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (T 1 , T 3 ) {tex} \in {/tex} R. Therefore, R is an equivalence relation on T.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: Two schools P and Q want to award their selected students on the values of Tolerance, Kindness, and Leadership. The school P wants to award ₹ x each, ₹ y each and ₹ z each for the three respective values to 3, 2 and 1 students respectively with total award money of ₹ 2200. School Q wants to spend ₹ 3100 to award its 4, 1 and 3 students on the respective values (by giving the same award money to the three values as school P). If the total amount of award for one prize on each value is ₹ 1200, using matrices, What is award money for Tolerance?
